perturbation.original_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [insert country of choice].
perturbation.explanation: By allowing for any country to be inserted, this change removes the specification for the laws of England and Wales. This is a problem if either party is based in the USA, because it would be expected that the agreement would be subject to the jurisdiction of the US Legal system. For example, if HealthGate is a US Company, it should be assumed that US Laws will be applicable to this contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC allows parties to choose applicable law if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state/country. If a US-based company is party to a contract, US law generally applies unless there's a reasonable basis for choosing a foreign law. Choosing an arbitrary 'country of choice' without a reasonable basis undermines the protections US law would otherwise provide.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by omitting the specific jurisdiction of England and Wales, and instead allowing for any country to be chosen. This contradicts the principle outlined in UCC § 1-301(a), which states that parties may choose the governing law if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to that state or nation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change is problematic if a party is based in the USA, because US law should apply. The Law Explanation suggests that the change undermines the protections US law would otherwise provide by allowing an arbitrary 'country of choice'. The omission of a defined jurisdiction, specifically England and Wales, opens the door to a potentially unreasonable choice of law, which could violate UCC § 1-301(a) if there is no reasonable relation to the chosen jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 The Material shall remain the confidential property of the Owner and in the event that NCC provides a copy of the Material to the Licensee, the Licensee shall be permitted to use the Material only in accordance with a confidentiality undertaking in the form contained in Schedule 3.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 The Material shall remain the confidential property of the Owner and in the event that NCC provides a copy of the Material to the Licensee, the Licensee shall be permitted to use the Material with no stipulations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the stipulation for a confidentiality agreement this exposes the original owner to a higher risk of IP theft, because there is nothing now in the contract to prevent the licensee from distributing the material. In the US, trade secrets law protects confidential business information, but relies on reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets. Omitting the confidentiality agreement removes a key 'reasonable measure' to protect the trade secret (the Material), potentially weakening the Owner's ability to claim trade secret misappropriation if the Licensee misuses the Material. If there is no confidentiality undertaking, the argument that reasonable measures have been taken to protect a trade secret is weakened.
perturbation.justification: The omission of a confidentiality agreement directly contradicts the need to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly detail these measures, it references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which implicitly requires such measures. Removing the confidentiality undertaking weakens the Owner's ability to claim trade secret misappropriation. The original text included a specific confidentiality undertaking, and the changed text eliminates this, creating a direct contradiction by removing a safeguard for confidential property.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 NCC shall not be liable for any loss caused to the Owner or the Licensee either jointly or severally except for loss of or damage to the Material to the extent that such loss or damage is caused by the negligent acts or omissions of NCC, its employees, agents or sub-contractors and in such event NCC's total liability in respect of all claims arising under or by virtue of this Agreement shall not (except in the case of claims for personal injury or death) exceed the sum of (pounds)500,000.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 NCC shall not be liable for any loss caused to the Owner or the Licensee either jointly or severally. In such event NCC's total liability in respect of all claims arising under or by virtue of this Agreement shall not exceed the sum of (pounds)100.
perturbation.explanation: This significantly limits NCC's liability to a mere £100 regardless of the damages incurred. In the United States, complete disclaimers of liability for negligence are often deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, particularly when dealing with essential services or involving significant disparity in bargaining power.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195. Unenforceability on Grounds of Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: This case law suggests that exculpatory clauses attempting to relieve a party from liability for intentional or grossly negligent conduct are unenforceable because they violate public policy. This change attempts to severely limit liability, potentially violating public policy if deemed unconscionable or against the public interest depending on jurisdiction and circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text limits NCC's liability to £500,000 for losses caused by their negligence, excluding personal injury or death claims. The changed text drastically reduces this limit to £100, removing the exception for negligence-caused damages. According to the Law Explanation, exculpatory clauses relieving a party from liability for grossly negligent conduct are unenforceable because they violate public policy. By limiting the liability to £100, the changed text attempts to relieve NCC from almost all liability, which contradicts public policy against allowing parties to escape liability for their own negligence.
